g4_revenue_trend.description: The issue in 1990 was credit panic around California real estate, not franchise decline. Buffett described Wells as a superbly managed, high-return banking operation.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: Bank margins were under stress from panic and provisioning, but the underlying franchise economics remained intact.

g7_moat.description: Wells Fargo combined a low-cost deposit franchise, disciplined management, and entrenched customer relationships in California banking. Buffett's 1990 letter framed it as a rare bank able to earn more than 20% on equity and 1.25% on assets, evidence of a real competitive advantage rather than a commodity lender surviving on leverage.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — larger institution, but without Wells Fargo's management quality and clean economics.
PASS — another California bank exposed to the same fear, but without the same proven earnings power and management edge.

buffett_action: Berkshire accumulated just under 10% of Wells Fargo in 1989-1990 for about $290M.
actual_result: A three-decade winner and one of Berkshire's canonical crisis purchases.
key_insight: Banking leverage is dangerous, but a panic can still create an extraordinary opportunity when franchise quality and management discipline are exceptional.
